CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-01-2020 Off Calendar

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-08-2020 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-23-2020 Continued To May-27-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-20-2020 Continued To Apr-08-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-22-2020 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance, or objection, the tentative ruling is adopted, modified to correct typos, as follows:

Cross-defendant The Yerba Mate Company's demurrer to cross-complainant's first cause of action for negligent hiring, supervision, and/or retention is sustained with twenty days leave to amend. "Liability for negligent hiring ... is based upon the reasoning that if an enterprise hires individuals with characteristics which might pose a danger to customers or other employees, the enterprise should bear the loss caused by the wrongdoing of its incompetent or unfit employees." (Mendoza v. City of Los Angeles (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 1333, 1339.) Negligence liability will be imposed on an employer if it "knew or should have known that hiring the employee created a particular risk or hazard and that particular harm materializes." (Doe v. Capital Cities (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1038, 1054.) Here, cross-complainant fails to allege any facts regarding what particular characteristics or propensities of Jimmerson gave Yerba notice that Jimmerson would engage in a fraudulent embezzlement scheme. Cross-complainant merely alleges that Jimmerson had an "extensive criminal history." There are no allegations that Jimmerson previously engaged in similar deceitful criminal conduct. Yerba's general knowledge that Jimmerson had a criminal history does not equate with knowledge that he would embark on a fraudulent embezzlement scheme. "In sum, the cornerstone of a negligent hiring theory is the risk that the employee will act in a certain way and the employee does act in that way. Plaintiff has failed to allege those necessary facts." (Doe v. Capital Cities, 50 Cal.App.4th 1038, 1054 [allegations that employer knew that employee used drugs and had used his prior position as casting director to gain sexual favors were insufficient to show that employer knew he would drug and then attack a potential employee].)

Yerba's demurrer to the negligent misrepresentation cause of action is sustained with twenty days leave to amend. "Each element in a cause of action for fraud or negligent misrepresentation must be factually and specifically alleged." (Cadio v. Owens-Illinois, Inc. (2004) 125 Cal.App.4th 513, 519.) In this case, Elgendy has leave to allege precisely how it was damaged. Elgendy cannot recover its fees in this action from Yerba under the tort of another doctrine. Yerba's duty argument fails. Jimmerson is alleged to be an employee/agent of Yerba and he owed a duty to speak truthfully. (See Stevens v. Torrengo (1961) 192 Cal.App.2d 105, 120 ["one who is asked for or volunteers information must be truthful, and that the telling of a half truth, calculated to deceive, is fraud."]; Esau v. Briggs (1948) 89 Cal.App.2d 427, 434-435 ["Even when a party is under no duty to speak as to a matter, if he does speak, he must speak the truth and 'make a full and fair disclosure.'"].)

Lastly, the court notes that Yerba did not provide a meet and confer declaration. The parties are advised that they must comply with the meet and confer requirements of Code of Civ. Proc. 430.41 before any future demurrers are filed.

The Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.
